old: This matter makes people feel it is mysterious.
new: This matter feels mysterious.
reason: Redundant 'it is' makes the translation awkward; more natural phrasing removes it.

old: The hot air balloon slowly rises up.
new: The hot air balloon slowly rises.
reason: 'Rises up' is redundant; 'rises' alone is correct.

old: The teacher's lecturing is very vivid.
new: The teacher's lessons are very vivid.
reason: 'The teacher's lecturing' is awkward English; 'lessons' is more natural.

old: 中国队在决赛中胜。
new: 中国队在决赛中胜了。
reason: The sentence is grammatically incomplete without the aspect marker 了; 胜 used as a standalone verb needs it here.

old: moist
new: wet
reason: All three sentences illustrate 'wet' (rain-soaked ground, sweat-soaked clothes, wet clothing), not 'moist'. The translations also consistently use 'wet'. The primary meaning of 湿 in these contexts is 'wet'.

old: The ground is very wet after rain.
new: The ground is very wet after it rains.
reason: Slightly more natural English phrasing.

old: He has remembered from beginning to end what his teacher said to him.
new: He has always remembered what his teacher said to him.
reason: Awkward word order in original; 始终 functions naturally as 'always' in this context.

old: He seems as if he's about to jump for joy.
new: He is so happy it seems as if he's about to jump up.
reason: 'For joy' is not in the Chinese (高兴得像要跳起来似的); the translation adds meaning not present in the source.

old: Every thing has two sides.
new: Everything has two sides.
reason: 'Every thing' should be one word 'everything' in this context.

old: She likes to make gifts done by hand.
new: She likes to make handmade gifts.
reason: 'Gifts done by hand' is an awkward calque; 'handmade gifts' is natural English.

old: He stayed to guard by the patient's side the whole time.
new: He stayed by the patient's side the whole time.
reason: Adding 'to guard' is not in the Chinese (他一直守在病人身边); 守 here means to keep watch/stay close, not military guarding.

old: Staying home is the most snug in winter.
new: In winter, staying home is the most snug.
reason: Awkward word order; reordering improves readability.

old: It's really happy to run into an acquaintance abroad.
new: Running into an acquaintance abroad is really delightful.
reason: 'It's really happy' is unnatural English; 'happy' cannot predicate an impersonal 'it' in this construction.

old: What he said has a lot of exaggeration.
new: What he said is greatly exaggerated.
reason: Unnatural phrasing; the idiomatic English rendering of 有很多水分 is 'is greatly exaggerated'.

old: Fruits contain a lot of moisture content.
new: Fruits contain a lot of moisture.
reason: 'Moisture content' as an object of 'contain' is redundant; 'moisture' alone is correct.

old: The weather is very hot and the skin lacks moisture content.
new: The weather is very hot and the skin lacks moisture.
reason: 'Lacks moisture content' is redundant; 'lacks moisture' is correct.

old: He went forward going along with the wind direction.
new: He walked forward with the wind.
reason: 'Going along with the wind direction' is an awkward calque of 顺风向; 'with the wind' is natural English.

old: without disorder
new: smoothly
reason: 'Without disorder' is an unnatural calque. All three sentences illustrate things proceeding smoothly/without problems, and the translations confirm this. 'Smoothly' is the natural English equivalent for this usage of 顺.

old: This project was completed without disorder.
new: This project went smoothly.
reason: Consistent with corrected definition; 'without disorder' is an unnatural calque.

old: The meeting proceeded without disorder.
new: The meeting proceeded very smoothly.
reason: Consistent with corrected definition; 'without disorder' is unnatural.

old: The event went without disorder — nothing went wrong.
new: The event went smoothly — nothing went wrong.
reason: Consistent with corrected definition.
